title: No-Go Theorem for Gaussian Quantum Repeaters from Fractional Extendibility
abstract: Photon loss in optical channels fundamentally limits long-range reliable quantum communication. A standard approach to overcoming this limitation is the use of quantum repeater nodes, which typically perform experimentally demanding non-Gaussian operations. However, whether Gaussian repeater protocols can enhance quantum communication rates over bosonic attenuation channels has remained open. In this work, we prove a no-go theorem for Gaussian quantum repeaters in a quantum network. Specifically, we show that any repeater chain composed of Gaussian operations, homodyne measurements, and arbitrary classical communication cannot enhance the quantum capacity of a pure-loss attenuation channel beyond that achievable by direct transmission. Our proof introduces a generalisation of $k$-extendibility to a notion of fractional extendibility for Gaussian states and establishes some of its useful properties, thereby providing a powerful framework for analysing Gaussian quantum networks.
summary: This paper establishes a no-go theorem proving that quantum repeaters using only Gaussian operations cannot improve the quantum capacity of a pure-loss optical channel beyond what direct transmission achieves. The authors achieve this by introducing and analyzing the mathematical concept of fractional extendibility for Gaussian states. This result places fundamental theoretical limits on the achievable rates in practical quantum communication networks.
main_problem: To prove a no-go theorem demonstrating that Gaussian quantum repeaters cannot enhance the quantum communication rate over a pure-loss bosonic attenuation channel.
main_result: The quantum capacity achieved by any repeater chain composed solely of Gaussian operations is upper-bounded by the quantum capacity achievable through direct transmission.
method: The proof introduces and utilizes the concept of fractional extendibility for Gaussian states, applying it within the framework of Gaussian quantum information theory.
model_or_system: The model involves quantum communication over optical channels limited by photon loss, analyzed using Gaussian states characterized by their first moment and Quantum Covariance Matrix (QCM).
key_observables: Quantum capacity $Q(A_\eta)$, the QCM of the output state ($V'_{A'}$), and the transmissivity $\eta$.
important_parameters: Transmissivity $\eta \in [0, 1]$, and the attenuation parameters $\gamma_i$ characterizing intermediate loss.
main_assumptions: The theorem is strictly limited to repeater chains restricted to performing only Gaussian operations (including homodyne measurements) and classical communication.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the Choi state of the repeater chain, showing the process involving intermediate attenuation channels and local operations. Figure 2 details how a bipartite state is prepared across three nodes using attenuation channels.
paper_structure: The work develops the mathematical framework by generalizing $k$-extendibility to fractional extendibility, proving key lemmas regarding its composition and monotonicity, and finally applying these tools to bound the quantum capacity of the full repeater chain $R_\eta$.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous mathematical boundary on the performance of quantum communication protocols when restricted to experimentally feasible Gaussian operations, which is highly relevant for quantum optics and quantum networking.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Choi state of the repeater chain, Rη corresponding to some message string m. γi’s denote attenuation parameters of the intermediate pure loss channels.

FIG. 2. Each repeater node prepares a bipartite state locally and shares one part to the next node using the attenuation channel (illustrated for three nodes). Thereafter they perform an arbitrary GLOCC protocol.
